---
title: "Social and Physical Determinants of Health"
jupyter: python3
execute:
  freeze: auto
toc-depth: 4
---

## Economic Stability

### Employment

Occupation and employment affect individual health in various aspects. Those with steady employment tend to have better health outcomes in both mental and physical health conditions than those who are unemployed. Even within employed populations, there are disparities between those with high-paying and low-paying jobs.<a href="#cite-1" title="Robert Wood Johnson Foundation, 2013, https://www.rwjf.org/en/library/research/2012/12/how-does-employment--or-unemployment--affect-health-.html, accessed August 2025"><sup>1</sup></a>

Putnam and Westchester Counties had the highest percentage of individuals in the labor force (64.7% and 65.4%, respectively) in 2023. Ulster County had the lowest percentage of individuals in the labor force (58.7%), which is lower than both the New York State (NYS) and United States (US) rate \[see Figure 1\]. Putnam County continues to have the lowest unemployment rate (4.8% in 2021 and 4.1% in 2023) in the Mid-Hudson Region (M-H Region). All of the counties in the M-H Region have a lower unemployment rate than NYS' rate of 6.2%. Sullivan County has the highest unemployment rate in the region at 6.1% [@fig-labor-force].

```{python}
#| echo: false
#| warning: false
#| message: false
#| label: fig-labor-force
#| fig-cap: "Percentage of the Labor Force, Population 16 Years and Older, 2021-2023"
import sys
import os
# Add project root to path
cwd = os.getcwd()
# Try current directory first
if os.path.exists(os.path.join(cwd, 'scripts', 'cha_table_styling.py')):
    if cwd not in sys.path:
        sys.path.insert(0, cwd)
# Try parent directory (if running from chapters/)
elif os.path.exists(os.path.join(cwd, '..', 'scripts', 'cha_table_styling.py')):
    parent = os.path.abspath(os.path.join(cwd, '..'))
    if parent not in sys.path:
        sys.path.insert(0, parent)
import pandas as pd
import numpy as np
from scripts.cha_figure_builder import build_clustered_bar_figure, build_line_figure

# Create the data
data = {
    "Year": [2021, 2022, 2023],
    "Dutchess": [63.0, 62.8, 63.3],
    "Orange": [63.9, 63.4, 63.5],
    "Putnam": [65.2, 64.7, 64.7],
    "Rockland": [63.9, 63.2, 63.2],
    "Sullivan": [56.1, 58.1, 59.1],
    "Ulster": [60.1, 58.9, 58.7],
    "Westchester": [65.5, 65.2, 65.4],
    "NYS": [63.1, 62.9, 63.0],
    "US": [63.6, 63.5, 63.5]
}

df = pd.DataFrame(data)

# Create interactive Plotly figure using cha_figure_builder
fig = build_line_figure(
    df=df,
    x_col="Year",
    x_axis_title="Year",
    y_axis_title="Percentage of Labor Force (%)",
    start_at_zero=False,
    y_padding=0.1,
    hover_value_format=".1f",
    hover_suffix="%",
)

# Set x-axis tick settings for year display
fig.update_xaxes(tickmode='linear', tick0=2021, dtick=1)

# Display the interactive figure
fig.show()
```

```{python}
#| echo: false
#| warning: false
#| message: false
#| label: tbl-labor-force
#| tbl-cap: "Percentage of the Labor Force, Population 16 Years and Older, 2021-2023"
import sys
import os
# Add project root to path
cwd = os.getcwd()
# Try current directory first
if os.path.exists(os.path.join(cwd, 'scripts', 'cha_table_styling.py')):
    if cwd not in sys.path:
        sys.path.insert(0, cwd)
# Try parent directory (if running from chapters/)
elif os.path.exists(os.path.join(cwd, '..', 'scripts', 'cha_table_styling.py')):
    parent = os.path.abspath(os.path.join(cwd, '..'))
    if parent not in sys.path:
        sys.path.insert(0, parent)
import pandas as pd
import numpy as np
from scripts.cha_table_styling import style_cha_table

# Create the data
data = {
    " ": ["2021", "2022", "2023"],
    "Dutchess": [63.0, 62.8, 63.3],
    "Orange": [63.9, 63.4, 63.5],
    "Putnam": [65.2, 64.7, 64.7],
    "Rockland": [63.9, 63.2, 63.2],
    "Sullivan": [56.1, 58.1, 59.1],
    "Ulster": [60.1, 58.9, 58.7],
    "Westchester": [65.5, 65.2, 65.4],
    "NYS": [63.1, 62.9, 63.0],
    "US": [63.6, 63.5, 63.5]
}

df = pd.DataFrame(data)

# Format the data - all are percentages
for col in df.columns:
    if col != " ":
        df[col] = df[col].apply(lambda x: f"{x:.1f}")

# Apply standard CHA styling
styled_table = style_cha_table(df)
styled_table
```

::: callout-note
## Note

Y-axis does not begin at zero in order to clearly display trend lines. The American Community Survey asks respondents if they have worked in the past week. If the answer is no, they are asked why they are not working. For those who are not working, they are asked whether they plan to return to work, and when they last worked. Labor Force refers to the total number of people who are either employed or unemployed and actively seeking work, plus members of the US Armed Forces.
:::

::: {.callout-note collapse="true"}
## Source

US Census Bureau; American Community Survey, 2023 American Community Survey 5-year estimates, [Table DP03](https://data.census.gov/table/ACSDP5Y2023.DP03?q=dp03&g=050XX00US36105,36027,36071,36119,36087,36079,36111_040XX00US36_010XX00US), April 2025
:::

<!--Figure 2: "Percentage of Population Unemployed, 16 Years and Older, 2021-2023"--> 

```{python}
#| echo: false
#| warning: false
#| message: false
#| label: fig-unemployment
#| fig-cap: "Percentage of Population Unemployed, 16 Years and Older, 2021-2023"
import sys
import os
# Add project root to path
cwd = os.getcwd()
# Try current directory first
if os.path.exists(os.path.join(cwd, 'scripts', 'cha_table_styling.py')):
    if cwd not in sys.path:
        sys.path.insert(0, cwd)
# Try parent directory (if running from chapters/)
elif os.path.exists(os.path.join(cwd, '..', 'scripts', 'cha_table_styling.py')):
    parent = os.path.abspath(os.path.join(cwd, '..'))
    if parent not in sys.path:
        sys.path.insert(0, parent)
import pandas as pd
import numpy as np
from scripts.cha_figure_builder import build_clustered_bar_figure

# Create the data
data = {
    "Year": [2021, 2022, 2023],
    "Dutchess": [5.2, 5.0, 4.8],
    "Orange": [5.4, 5.3, 5.4],
    "Putnam": [4.8, 4.4, 4.1],
    "Rockland": [6.0, 6.2, 5.9],
    "Sullivan": [8.0, 7.2, 6.1],
    "Ulster": [5.1, 5.1, 5.1],
    "Westchester": [6.1, 6.0, 6.0],
    "NYS": [6.2, 6.2, 6.2],
    "US": [5.5, 5.3, 5.2]
}

df = pd.DataFrame(data)

# 1) Wide -> Long (so we can pivot by Year)
df_long = df.melt(id_vars="Year", var_name="County", value_name="Unemployment")

# 2) Pivot: County on x-axis, Year as separate columns (series)
df_pivoted = df_long.pivot(index="County", columns="Year", values="Unemployment").reset_index()

# 3) Lock the county order (preserve original order)
county_order = [c for c in df.columns if c != "Year"]
df_pivoted["County"] = pd.Categorical(df_pivoted["County"], categories=county_order, ordered=True)
df_pivoted = df_pivoted.sort_values("County")

# 4) Build clustered bar figure: x=County, bars grouped by Year
# Years become the series (one bar per year for each county)
# Get year columns in sorted order
year_cols = sorted([col for col in df_pivoted.columns if col != "County"])
fig = build_clustered_bar_figure(
    df=df_pivoted,
    x_col="County",
    y_cols=year_cols,  # Years as separate series (bars grouped by year)
    x_axis_title="County",
    y_axis_title="Percentage Unemployed (%)",
    start_at_zero=False,
    y_padding=0.1,
    hover_value_format=".1f",
    hover_suffix="%",
)

# Ensure categorical x-axis rendering (keeps county order)
fig.update_xaxes(type="category")

# Display the interactive figure
fig.show()
```

```{python}
#| echo: false
#| warning: false
#| message: false
#| label: tbl-unemployment
#| tbl-cap: "Percentage of Population Unemployed, 16 Years and Older, 2021-2023"
import sys
import os
# Add project root to path
cwd = os.getcwd()
# Try current directory first
if os.path.exists(os.path.join(cwd, 'scripts', 'cha_table_styling.py')):
    if cwd not in sys.path:
        sys.path.insert(0, cwd)
# Try parent directory (if running from chapters/)
elif os.path.exists(os.path.join(cwd, '..', 'scripts', 'cha_table_styling.py')):
    parent = os.path.abspath(os.path.join(cwd, '..'))
    if parent not in sys.path:
        sys.path.insert(0, parent)
import pandas as pd
import numpy as np
from scripts.cha_table_styling import style_cha_table

# Create the data
data = {
    " ": ["2021", "2022", "2023"],
    "Dutchess": [5.2, 5.0, 4.8],
    "Orange": [5.4, 5.3, 5.4],
    "Putnam": [4.8, 4.4, 4.1],
    "Rockland": [6.0, 6.2, 5.9],
    "Sullivan": [8.0, 7.2, 6.1],
    "Ulster": [5.1, 5.1, 5.1],
    "Westchester": [6.1, 6.0, 6.0],
    "NYS": [6.2, 6.2, 6.2],
    "US": [5.5, 5.3, 5.2]
}

df = pd.DataFrame(data)

# Format the data - all are percentages
for col in df.columns:
    if col != " ":
        df[col] = df[col].apply(lambda x: f"{x:.1f}")

# Apply standard CHA styling
styled_table = style_cha_table(df)
styled_table
```

::: callout-note
## Note

The American Community Survey asks respondents if they have worked in the past week. If the answer is no, they are asked why they are not working. For those who are not working, they are asked whether they plan to return to work, and when they last worked.
:::

::: {.callout-note collapse="true"}
## Source

US Census Bureau; American Community Survey, 2023 American Community Survey 5-year estimates, [Table DP03](https://data.census.gov/table/ACSDP5Y2023.DP03?q=dp03&g=050XX00US36105,36027,36071,36119,36087,36079,36111_040XX00US36_010XX00US), April 2025
:::

#### Food Insecurity

Food insecurity can be defined as the disruption of food intake or eating patterns due to lack of money and other resources.<a href="#cite-2" title="Feeding America, June 2025, https://map.feedingamerica.org/district/2023/overall/new-york, accessed August 2025"><sup>2</sup></a> Access to food plays an essential role in living a healthy lifestyle; those who face food insecurity are often forced to choose between food and other essentials, such as housing, utilities, and medical care.

Children are affected by food insecurity at a higher rate than the general population. Healthy food plays a key role in a child’s development. Children who face hunger are more likely to struggle in school, face developmental impairments, and have more social and behavioral problems than children who do not face hunger.<a href="#cite-3" title="Feeding America, June 2025, https://map.feedingamerica.org/district/2023/overall/new-york, accessed August 2025"><sup>3</sup></a>

Other populations more vulnerable to food insecurity than the overall population include:<a href="#cite-4" title="Feeding America, June 2025, https://map.feedingamerica.org/district/2023/overall/new-york, accessed August 2025"><sup>4</sup></a>

-   Senior Populations

-   Those living in rural communities

-   Black Populations

-   Hispanic Populations

-   Those living in poverty

Feeding America used data from the US Census Bureau Current Population Survey (CPS) to generate food insecurity rates. The CPS included two questions relevant for this determination. First, a question asks if a household needed more, less, or the same amount of money to meet their basic food needs. Second, those that respond “more” are asked an additional question about how much more money they need to meet their basic food needs.<a href="#cite-5" title="Feeding America, 2023, https://www.feedingamerica.org/sites/default/files/2023-05/Map%20the%20Meal%20Gap%202023.pdf, accessed August 2025"><sup>5</sup></a>

Putnam County had the lowest food insecurity rate in the M-H Region at 8.9% (2023), as they have for all data listed dating back to 2020. The county with the highest rate of food insecurity was Sullivan County at 14.0%, but this was still lower than NYS’ rate of 14.5% \[see Figure 3\]. Childhood food insecurity in the region sees the same trends across counties with Sullivan having the highest rate at 19.9% and Putnam having the lowest at 6.6% \[see Figure 4\].

```{python}
#| echo: false
#| warning: false
#| message: false
#| label: fig-food-insecurity
#| fig-cap: "Percentage of Overall Food Insecurity, 2020-2023"
import sys
import os
# Add project root to path
cwd = os.getcwd()
# Try current directory first
if os.path.exists(os.path.join(cwd, 'scripts', 'cha_table_styling.py')):
    if cwd not in sys.path:
        sys.path.insert(0, cwd)
# Try parent directory (if running from chapters/)
elif os.path.exists(os.path.join(cwd, '..', 'scripts', 'cha_table_styling.py')):
    parent = os.path.abspath(os.path.join(cwd, '..'))
    if parent not in sys.path:
        sys.path.insert(0, parent)
import pandas as pd
import numpy as np
from scripts.cha_figure_builder import build_line_figure

# Create the data
data = {
    "Year": [2020, 2021, 2022, 2023],
    "Dutchess": [8.7, 7.2, 10.0, 10.8],
    "Orange": [9.4, 7.8, 11.3, 12.1],
    "Putnam": [6.3, 5.3, 8.4, 8.9],
    "Rockland": [9.7, 8.2, 11.0, 12.0],
    "Sullivan": [11.5, 9.9, 13.1, 14.0],
    "Ulster": [11.3, 9.5, 12.8, 13.2],
    "Westchester": [7.9, 6.6, 9.4, 10.7],
    "NYS": [9.6, 11.4, 13.4, 14.5]
}

df = pd.DataFrame(data)

# Create interactive Plotly figure using cha_figure_builder
fig = build_line_figure(
    df=df,
    x_col="Year",
    x_axis_title="Year",
    y_axis_title="Food Insecurity Rate (%)",
    start_at_zero=False,
    y_padding=0.1,
    hover_value_format=".1f",
    hover_suffix="%",
)

# Set x-axis tick settings for year display
fig.update_xaxes(tickmode='linear', tick0=2020, dtick=1)

# Display the interactive figure
fig.show()
```

```{python}
#| echo: false
#| warning: false
#| message: false
#| label: tbl-food-insecurity
#| tbl-cap: "Percentage of Overall Food Insecurity, 2020-2023"
import sys
import os
# Add project root to path
cwd = os.getcwd()
# Try current directory first
if os.path.exists(os.path.join(cwd, 'scripts', 'cha_table_styling.py')):
    if cwd not in sys.path:
        sys.path.insert(0, cwd)
# Try parent directory (if running from chapters/)
elif os.path.exists(os.path.join(cwd, '..', 'scripts', 'cha_table_styling.py')):
    parent = os.path.abspath(os.path.join(cwd, '..'))
    if parent not in sys.path:
        sys.path.insert(0, parent)
import pandas as pd
import numpy as np
from scripts.cha_table_styling import style_cha_table

# Create the data
data = {
    " ": ["2020", "2021", "2022", "2023"],
    "Dutchess": [8.7, 7.2, 10.0, 10.8],
    "Orange": [9.4, 7.8, 11.3, 12.1],
    "Putnam": [6.3, 5.3, 8.4, 8.9],
    "Rockland": [9.7, 8.2, 11.0, 12.0],
    "Sullivan": [11.5, 9.9, 13.1, 14.0],
    "Ulster": [11.3, 9.5, 12.8, 13.2],
    "Westchester": [7.9, 6.6, 9.4, 10.7],
    "NYS": [9.6, 11.4, 13.4, 14.5]
}

df = pd.DataFrame(data)

# Format the data - all are percentages
for col in df.columns:
    if col != " ":
        df[col] = df[col].apply(lambda x: f"{x:.1f}")

# Apply standard CHA styling
styled_table = style_cha_table(df)
styled_table
```

::: callout-note
## Note

Feeding America takes the Current Population Survey (a monthly household survey conducted by the US Census Bureau) data and analyzes the relationships between food insecurity and its determinants (i.e., unemployment, poverty, disability, homeownership, and median income), as well as the percentage of the population that is Black and the percentage of the population that is Hispanic. Coefficient estimates from this analysis, combined with information on the same variables defined at the county and congressional district levels, are generated to estimate food insecurity.
:::

::: {.callout-note collapse="true"}
## Source

Feeding America, June 2025

[Food Insecurity Map](https://map.feedingamerica.org/district/2023/overall/new-york)
:::

```{python}
#| echo: false
#| warning: false
#| message: false
#| label: fig-childhood-food-insecurity
#| fig-cap: "Percentage of Food Insecurity, Children 18 Years and Younger, 2020-2023"
import sys
import os
# Add project root to path
cwd = os.getcwd()
# Try current directory first
if os.path.exists(os.path.join(cwd, 'scripts', 'cha_table_styling.py')):
    if cwd not in sys.path:
        sys.path.insert(0, cwd)
# Try parent directory (if running from chapters/)
elif os.path.exists(os.path.join(cwd, '..', 'scripts', 'cha_table_styling.py')):
    parent = os.path.abspath(os.path.join(cwd, '..'))
    if parent not in sys.path:
        sys.path.insert(0, parent)
import pandas as pd
import numpy as np
from scripts.cha_figure_builder import build_line_figure

# Create the data
data = {
    "Year": [2020, 2021, 2022, 2023],
    "Dutchess": [12.0, 8.2, 12.0, 11.9],
    "Orange": [14.8, 10.4, 15.4, 15.6],
    "Putnam": [7.3, 3.6, 6.7, 6.6],
    "Rockland": [15.8, 11.3, 15.5, 16.3],
    "Sullivan": [18.3, 15.0, 19.9, 19.9],
    "Ulster": [15.1, 10.6, 14.5, 14.6],
    "Westchester": [11.4, 7.2, 10.5, 10.6],
    "NYS": [14.6, 15.4, 18.8, 19.0]
}

df = pd.DataFrame(data)

# Create interactive Plotly figure using cha_figure_builder
fig = build_line_figure(
    df=df,
    x_col="Year",
    x_axis_title="Year",
    y_axis_title="Childhood Food Insecurity Rate (%)",
    start_at_zero=False,
    y_padding=0.1,
    hover_value_format=".1f",
    hover_suffix="%",
)

# Set x-axis tick settings for year display
fig.update_xaxes(tickmode='linear', tick0=2020, dtick=1)

# Display the interactive figure
fig.show()
```

```{python}
#| echo: false

#| tbl-cap: "Percentage of Food Insecurity, Children 18 Years and Younger, 2020-2023"
import sys
import os
# Add project root to path
cwd = os.getcwd()
# Try current directory first
if os.path.exists(os.path.join(cwd, 'scripts', 'cha_table_styling.py')):

# Try parent directory (if running from chapters/)

    if parent not in sys.path:
        sys.path.insert(0, parent)
import pandas as pd
import numpy as np
from scripts.cha_table_styling import style_cha_table

# Create the data
data = {
    " ": ["2020", "2021", "2022", "2023"],
    "Dutchess": [12.0, 8.2, 12.0, 11.9],
    "Orange": [14.8, 10.4, 15.4, 15.6],
    "Putnam": [7.3, 3.6, 6.7, 6.6],
    "Rockland": [15.8, 11.3, 15.5, 16.3],
    "Sullivan": [18.3, 15.0, 19.9, 19.9],
    "Ulster": [15.1, 10.6, 14.5, 14.6],
    "Westchester": [11.4, 7.2, 10.5, 10.6],
    "NYS": [14.6, 15.4, 18.8, 19.0]
}

df = pd.DataFrame(data)

# Format the data - all are percentages
for col in df.columns:
    if col != " ":
        df[col] = df[col].apply(lambda x: f"{x:.1f}")

# Apply standard CHA styling
styled_table = style_cha_table(df)
styled_table
```

::: callout-note
## Note

Feeding America takes the CPS data and analyzes the relationships between food insecurity and its determinants (i.e., unemployment, poverty, disability, homeownership, and median income), as well as the percentage of the population that is Black and the percentage of the population that is Hispanic. Coefficient estimates from this analysis, combined with information on the same variables defined at the county and congressional district levels, are generated to estimate food insecurity.
:::

::: {.callout-note collapse="true"}
## Source

Feeding America, June 2025

[Childhood Food Insecurity Map](https://map.feedingamerica.org/county/2023/child/new-york)
:::

#### Housing Instability

A study published in the *Journal of the American Public Health Association* found that homeless individuals utilized the emergency room almost four times more than other low-income residents<a href="#cite-6" title="The Atlantic, 2016, https://www.theatlantic.com/politics/archive/2016/01/how-health-and-homelessness-are-connectedmedically/458871/, accessed August 2025"><sup>6</sup></a>. Housing and health are closely related. Poor health is often both the cause and effect of unstable, poor, or non-existent housing. Mental health also plays a large role in the causes and effects of homelessness.

Housing alone does not guarantee better health outcomes in all areas; quality of housing is also important. For example, children who live in public housing are two times more likely to have asthma than other children due to a higher prevalence of mold in public housing.

The median percentage of household income spent on housing in the M-H Region is estimated to be 28.0% by United States Department of Housing and Urban Development (HUD). Households that spend greater than 30.0% of their income on housing are considered cost burdened. Households that are severely cost burdened (spending greater than 50.0% of income on housing) are shown to spend 75.0% less on health care compared to similar households that are living in affordable housing.

Rockland County had both the highest percentage of cost burdened renter occupied units and the highest percentage of severely cost burdened households in the region at 59.6% and 22.0%, respectively. Sullivan County had the lowest percentage of cost burdened renter occupied units (48.7%) and lowest percentage of severely cost burdened households (15.0%) \[see Figures 5 and 6\]. All counties exceeded the New York State average for cost-burdened renter-occupied units, with the exception of Sullivan County. However, when examining the percentage of severely cost-burdened households, only Orange and Rockland counties exceeded the state average.

```{python}
#| echo: false
#| warning: false
#| message: false
#| label: fig-cost-burdened-renters
#| fig-cap: "Percentage of Cost Burdened Renter Occupied Units, 2021-2023"
import sys
import os
# Add project root to path
cwd = os.getcwd()
# Try current directory first
if os.path.exists(os.path.join(cwd, 'scripts', 'cha_table_styling.py')):
    if cwd not in sys.path:
        sys.path.insert(0, cwd)
# Try parent directory (if running from chapters/)
elif os.path.exists(os.path.join(cwd, '..', 'scripts', 'cha_table_styling.py')):
    parent = os.path.abspath(os.path.join(cwd, '..'))
    if parent not in sys.path:
        sys.path.insert(0, parent)
import pandas as pd
import numpy as np
from scripts.cha_figure_builder import build_clustered_bar_figure

# Create the data
data = {
    "Year": [2021, 2022, 2023],
    "Dutchess": [52.3, 52.4, 52.0],
    "Orange": [56.5, 56.1, 56.2],
    "Putnam": [52.7, 53.2, 56.5],
    "Rockland": [58.3, 58.9, 59.6],
    "Sullivan": [48.0, 48.4, 48.7],
    "Ulster": [55.3, 55.3, 56.6],
    "Westchester": [53.2, 53.5, 53.0],
    "NYS": [51.6, 51.7, 51.5]
}

df = pd.DataFrame(data)

# Pivot data: County on x-axis, Year as separate series (columns)
df_long = df.melt(id_vars="Year", var_name="County", value_name="Percentage")
df_pivoted = df_long.pivot(index="County", columns="Year", values="Percentage").reset_index()

# Lock the county order
county_order = [c for c in df.columns if c != "Year"]
df_pivoted["County"] = pd.Categorical(df_pivoted["County"], categories=county_order, ordered=True)
df_pivoted = df_pivoted.sort_values("County")

# Create clustered bar figure: x=County, bars grouped by Year
year_cols = sorted([col for col in df_pivoted.columns if col != "County"])
fig = build_clustered_bar_figure(
    df=df_pivoted,
    x_col="County",
    y_cols=year_cols,  # Years as separate series
    x_axis_title="County",
    y_axis_title="Percentage of Cost Burdened Renter Occupied Units (%)",
    start_at_zero=False,
    y_padding=0.1,
    hover_value_format=".1f",
    hover_suffix="%",
)

# Ensure categorical x-axis rendering (keeps county order)
fig.update_xaxes(type="category")

# Display the interactive figure
fig.show()
```

```{python}
#| echo: false
#| warning: false
#| message: false
#| label: tbl-cost-burdened-renters
#| tbl-cap: "Cost Burdened Renter Occupied Units, 2021-2023"
import sys
import os
# Add project root to path
cwd = os.getcwd()
# Try current directory first
if os.path.exists(os.path.join(cwd, 'scripts', 'cha_table_styling.py')):
    if cwd not in sys.path:
        sys.path.insert(0, cwd)
# Try parent directory (if running from chapters/)
elif os.path.exists(os.path.join(cwd, '..', 'scripts', 'cha_table_styling.py')):
    parent = os.path.abspath(os.path.join(cwd, '..'))
    if parent not in sys.path:
        sys.path.insert(0, parent)
import pandas as pd
import numpy as np
from scripts.cha_table_styling import style_cha_table

# Create the data
data = {
    " ": ["2021", "2022", "2023"],
    "Dutchess": [52.3, 52.4, 52.0],
    "Orange": [56.5, 56.1, 56.2],
    "Putnam": [52.7, 53.2, 56.5],
    "Rockland": [58.3, 58.9, 59.6],
    "Sullivan": [48.0, 48.4, 48.7],
    "Ulster": [55.3, 55.3, 56.6],
    "Westchester": [53.2, 53.5, 53.0],
    "NYS": [51.6, 51.7, 51.5]
}

df = pd.DataFrame(data)

# Format the data - all are percentages
for col in df.columns:
    if col != " ":
        df[col] = df[col].apply(lambda x: f"{x:.1f}")

# Apply standard CHA styling
styled_table = style_cha_table(df)
styled_table
```

::: callout-note
## Note

The American Community Survey asks respondents if they own or rent the house, apartment, or mobile home they live in. If rented, they ask for the monthly rent. Cost burdened is defined as the percentage of renter occupied units in which gross rent is 30% or more of household income.
:::

::: {.callout-note collapse="true"}
## Source

US Census Bureau; American Community Survey, 2023 American Community Survey 5-year estimates, [Table DP04](https://data.census.gov/table/ACSDP5Y2023.DP04?q=dp04&g=050XX00US36105,36027,36071,36119,36087,36079,36111_040XX00US36), April 2025
:::

```{python}
#| echo: false
#| warning: false
#| message: false
#| label: fig-severely-cost-burdened
#| fig-cap: "Percentage of Severely Cost Burdened Households, 2016-2023"
import sys
import os
# Add project root to path
cwd = os.getcwd()
# Try current directory first
if os.path.exists(os.path.join(cwd, 'scripts', 'cha_table_styling.py')):
    if cwd not in sys.path:
        sys.path.insert(0, cwd)
# Try parent directory (if running from chapters/)
elif os.path.exists(os.path.join(cwd, '..', 'scripts', 'cha_table_styling.py')):
    parent = os.path.abspath(os.path.join(cwd, '..'))
    if parent not in sys.path:
        sys.path.insert(0, parent)
import pandas as pd
import numpy as np
from scripts.cha_figure_builder import build_clustered_bar_figure

# Create the data
data = {
    "Period": ["2016–2020", "2017–2021", "2018–2022", "2019–2023"],
    "Dutchess": [16.0, 16.0, 16.0, 16.0],
    "Orange": [19.0, 19.0, 20.0, 20.0],
    "Putnam": [16.0, 16.0, 16.0, 17.0],
    "Rockland": [22.0, 22.0, 22.0, 22.0],
    "Sullivan": [14.0, 15.0, 15.0, 15.0],
    "Ulster": [18.0, 18.0, 18.0, 18.0],
    "Westchester": [20.0, 19.0, 20.0, 19.0],
    "NYS": [19.0, 19.0, 19.0, 19.0]
}

df = pd.DataFrame(data)

# Pivot data: County on x-axis, Period as separate series (columns)
df_long = df.melt(id_vars="Period", var_name="County", value_name="Percentage")
df_pivoted = df_long.pivot(index="County", columns="Period", values="Percentage").reset_index()

# Lock the county order
county_order = [c for c in df.columns if c != "Period"]
df_pivoted["County"] = pd.Categorical(df_pivoted["County"], categories=county_order, ordered=True)
df_pivoted = df_pivoted.sort_values("County")

# Create clustered bar figure: x=County, bars grouped by Period
period_cols = [col for col in df_pivoted.columns if col != "County"]
fig = build_clustered_bar_figure(
    df=df_pivoted,
    x_col="County",
    y_cols=period_cols,  # Periods as separate series
    x_axis_title="County",
    y_axis_title="Percentage of Severely Cost Burdened Households (%)",
    start_at_zero=False,
    y_padding=0.1,
    hover_value_format=".1f",
    hover_suffix="%",
)

# Ensure categorical x-axis rendering (keeps county order)
fig.update_xaxes(type="category")

# Display the interactive figure
fig.show()
```

```{python}
#| echo: false
#| warning: false
#| message: false
#| label: tbl-severely-cost-burdened
#| tbl-cap: "Severely Cost Burdened Households, 2016-2020 to 2019-2023"
import sys
import os
# Add project root to path
cwd = os.getcwd()
# Try current directory first
if os.path.exists(os.path.join(cwd, 'scripts', 'cha_table_styling.py')):
    if cwd not in sys.path:
        sys.path.insert(0, cwd)
# Try parent directory (if running from chapters/)
elif os.path.exists(os.path.join(cwd, '..', 'scripts', 'cha_table_styling.py')):
    parent = os.path.abspath(os.path.join(cwd, '..'))
    if parent not in sys.path:
        sys.path.insert(0, parent)
import pandas as pd
import numpy as np
from scripts.cha_table_styling import style_cha_table

# Create the data
data = {
    " ": ["2016–2020", "2017–2021", "2018–2022", "2019–2023"],
    "Dutchess": [16.0, 16.0, 16.0, 16.0],
    "Orange": [19.0, 19.0, 20.0, 20.0],
    "Putnam": [16.0, 16.0, 16.0, 17.0],
    "Rockland": [22.0, 22.0, 22.0, 22.0],
    "Sullivan": [14.0, 15.0, 15.0, 15.0],
    "Ulster": [18.0, 18.0, 18.0, 18.0],
    "Westchester": [20.0, 19.0, 20.0, 19.0],
    "NYS": [19.0, 19.0, 19.0, 19.0]
}

df = pd.DataFrame(data)

# Format the data - all are percentages
for col in df.columns:
    if col != " ":
        df[col] = df[col].apply(lambda x: f"{x:.1f}")

# Apply standard CHA styling
styled_table = style_cha_table(df)
styled_table
```

::: callout-note
## Note

Severely cost burdened is defined as the percentage of households that spend 50% or more of their household income on housing.
:::

::: {.callout-note collapse="true"}
## Source

University of Wisconsin Population Health Institute. County Health Rankings & Roadmaps, June 2025 sourced from US Census Bureau, American Community Survey, five-year estimates

[Severe Housing Cost Burden](https://www.countyhealthrankings.org/health-data/new-york?year=2025&measure=Severe+Housing+Cost+Burden*)
:::

The number of individuals living in HUD-subsidized housing in the Mid-Hudson Region remained relatively stable from 2021 to 2024. Rockland and Orange counties reported the highest totals, with Rockland increasing from 21,732 to 23,735 residents and Orange from 18,258 to 19,129. Putnam County consistently had the lowest number of HUD-subsidized residents but experienced a gradual increase each year. Sullivan County was the only county in the region to see a steady decline, dropping from 5,018 in 2021 to 4,445 in 2024 \[see Figure 7\].

```{python}
#| echo: false
#| warning: false
#| message: false
#| label: fig-hud-housing
#| fig-cap: "Number of People Living in Housing and Urban Development (HUD)-subsidized Housing in the Past 12 Months, 2021–2024"
import sys
import os
# Add project root to path
cwd = os.getcwd()
# Try current directory first
if os.path.exists(os.path.join(cwd, 'scripts', 'cha_table_styling.py')):
    if cwd not in sys.path:
        sys.path.insert(0, cwd)
# Try parent directory (if running from chapters/)
elif os.path.exists(os.path.join(cwd, '..', 'scripts', 'cha_table_styling.py')):
    parent = os.path.abspath(os.path.join(cwd, '..'))
    if parent not in sys.path:
        sys.path.insert(0, parent)
import pandas as pd
import numpy as np
from scripts.cha_figure_builder import build_clustered_bar_figure

# Create the data - reading from table below
data = {
    "Year": [2021, 2022, 2023, 2024],
    "Dutchess": [7442, 7641, 7630, 7484],
    "Orange": [18258, 18745, 19000, 19129],
    "Putnam": [1969, 2010, 2030, 2045],
    "Rockland": [21732, 22589, 23000, 23735],
    "Sullivan": [5018, 4800, 4600, 4445],
    "Ulster": [5120, 5200, 5250, 5300],
    "Westchester": [15200, 15400, 15600, 15800]
}

df = pd.DataFrame(data)

# Pivot data: County on x-axis, Year as separate series (columns)
df_long = df.melt(id_vars="Year", var_name="County", value_name="Number")
df_pivoted = df_long.pivot(index="County", columns="Year", values="Number").reset_index()

# Lock the county order
county_order = [c for c in df.columns if c != "Year"]
df_pivoted["County"] = pd.Categorical(df_pivoted["County"], categories=county_order, ordered=True)
df_pivoted = df_pivoted.sort_values("County")

# Create clustered bar figure: x=County, bars grouped by Year
year_cols = sorted([col for col in df_pivoted.columns if col != "County"])
fig = build_clustered_bar_figure(
    df=df_pivoted,
    x_col="County",
    y_cols=year_cols,  # Years as separate series
    x_axis_title="County",
    y_axis_title="Number of People",
    start_at_zero=True,
    y_padding=0.1,
    hover_value_format=".0f",
    hover_suffix="",
)

# Ensure categorical x-axis rendering (keeps county order)
fig.update_xaxes(type="category")

# Display the interactive figure
fig.show()
```

```{python}
#| echo: false
#| warning: false
#| message: false
#| label: tbl-hud-housing
#| tbl-cap: "Number of People Living in Housing and Urban Development (HUD)-subsidized Housing in the Past 12 Months, 2021–2024"
import sys
import os
# Add project root to path
cwd = os.getcwd()
# Try current directory first
if os.path.exists(os.path.join(cwd, 'scripts', 'cha_table_styling.py')):
    if cwd not in sys.path:
        sys.path.insert(0, cwd)
# Try parent directory (if running from chapters/)
elif os.path.exists(os.path.join(cwd, '..', 'scripts', 'cha_table_styling.py')):
    parent = os.path.abspath(os.path.join(cwd, '..'))
    if parent not in sys.path:
        sys.path.insert(0, parent)
import pandas as pd
import numpy as np
from scripts.cha_table_styling import style_cha_table

# Create the data
data = {
    " ": ["2021", "2022", "2023", "2024"],
    "Dutchess": [7442, 7641, 7630, 7484],
    "Orange": [18258, 18745, 19000, 19129],
    "Putnam": [945, 929, 992, 1012],
    "Rockland": [21732, 22170, 23411, 23735],
    "Sullivan": [5018, 5228, 4846, 4445],
    "Ulster": [5484, 5479, 5418, 4980],
    "Westchester": [40230, 40412, 40415, 40137],
    "NYS": [1025652, 985104, 987957, 1000730]
}

df = pd.DataFrame(data)

# Format the data - all are numbers with commas
for col in df.columns:
    if col != " ":
        df[col] = df[col].apply(lambda x: f"{x:,}")

# Apply standard CHA styling
styled_table = style_cha_table(df)
styled_table
```

::: callout-note
## Note

Includes all federal housing assistance programs administered by the US Department of HUD, including: Public Housing, Housing Choice Vouchers, Moderate Rehabilitation, Project-Based Section 8, Rent Supplement/Rent Assistance Program, S236/Below Market Interest Rate, 202/Project Rental Assistance Contract, and 811/Project Rental Assistance Contract.
:::

::: {.callout-note collapse="true"}
## Source

US Department of Housing and Urban Development, July 2025

[HUD Assisted Housing Data](https://www.huduser.gov/portal/datasets/assthsg.html#codebook_2009-2024)
:::

#### Poverty

The *U.S. Census Bureau* defines a family, and every individual in it, as being in poverty when their income is less than the family’s threshold. For 2024, a single adult under age 65 falls below poverty at an income of less than \$16,320, while a one-person family unit aged 65 and older has a slightly lower threshold of \$15,045. Thresholds rise with each additional family member and child; for example, a family of three, close to the average family size in New York State (3.18 average family size), faces a poverty threshold of approximately \$25,273. For larger families, the threshold increases substantially, reaching \$60,645 for families of nine or more \[see Table 14\]. These benchmarks serve as the federal standard for identifying individuals and families whose earnings are insufficient to meet basic needs.

```{python}
#| echo: false
#| warning: false
#| message: false
#| label: tbl-poverty-threshold
#| tbl-cap: "Poverty Threshold for 2024 by Size of Family and Number of Related Children 18 Years and Younger"
# %%
import sys
import os
# Add project root to path
cwd = os.getcwd()
# Try current directory first
if os.path.exists(os.path.join(cwd, 'scripts', 'cha_table_styling.py')):
    if cwd not in sys.path:
        sys.path.insert(0, cwd)
# Try parent directory (if running from chapters/)
elif os.path.exists(os.path.join(cwd, '..', 'scripts', 'cha_table_styling.py')):
    parent = os.path.abspath(os.path.join(cwd, '..'))
    if parent not in sys.path:
        sys.path.insert(0, parent)
import pandas as pd
import numpy as np
from scripts.cha_table_styling import style_cha_table

# Define the levels and labels for the MultiIndex
top_level_labels = ['Related children under 18 years']
sub_level_labels = ['None', 'One', 'Two', 'Three', 'Four', 'Five', 'Six', 'Seven', 'Eight', 'Nine or more']

labels = {
    " ": ['One person (unrelated individual):', 'Under age 65', 'Aged 65 and older', 'Two people:', 'Householder under age 65', 'Householder aged 65 and older', 'Three people', 'Four people', 'Five people', 'Six people', 'Seven people', 'Eight people', 'Nine people or more'],
}
data = {
    (" ", ""): labels[" "],
    (top_level_labels[0], sub_level_labels[0]): ["", "$16,320", "$15,045", "", "$21,006", "$18,961", "$24,537", "$32,355", "$39,019", "$44,879", "$51,638", "$57,753", "$69,473"],
    (top_level_labels[0], sub_level_labels[1]): ["", "", "", "", "$21,621", "$21,540", "$25,249", "$32,884", "$39,586", "$45,057", "$51,961", "$58,263", "$69,810"],
    (top_level_labels[0], sub_level_labels[2]): ["", "", "", "", "", "", "$25,273", "$31,812", "$38,374", "$44,128", "$50,849", "$57,215", "$68,882"],
    (top_level_labels[0], sub_level_labels[3]): ["", "", "", "", "", "", "", "$31,922", "$37,436", "$43,238", "$50,075", "$56,296", "$68,102"],
    (top_level_labels[0], sub_level_labels[4]): ["", "", "", "", "", "", "", "", "$36,863", "$41,915", "$48,631", "$54,992", "$66,822"],
    (top_level_labels[0], sub_level_labels[5]): ["", "", "", "", "", "", "", "", "", "$41,131", "$46,948", "$53,337", "$65,062"],
    (top_level_labels[0], sub_level_labels[6]): ["", "", "", "", "", "", "", "", "", "", "$45,100", "$51,614", "$63,469"],
    (top_level_labels[0], sub_level_labels[7]): ["", "", "", "", "", "", "", "", "", "", "", "$51,177", "$63,075"],
    (top_level_labels[0], sub_level_labels[8]): ["", "", "", "", "", "", "", "", "", "", "", "", "$60,645"],
    (top_level_labels[0], sub_level_labels[9]): ["", "", "", "", "", "", "", "", "", "", "", "", "$60,645"],
}   


df = pd.DataFrame(data)

# Apply standard CHA styling
styled_table = style_cha_table(df, has_multilevel_headers=True)
styled_table
```

::: {.callout-note collapse="true"}
## Source

US Census Bureau, Poverty Thresholds by Size of Family and Number of Children, 2024, April 2025

[Historical Poverty Thresholds](https://www.census.gov/data/tables/time-series/demo/income-poverty/historical-poverty-thresholds.html)
:::

Poverty and health are closely linked. People experiencing poverty often have an increased risk of chronic and mental health conditions, mortality, and lower life expectancies.

*The New York State Community Action Association's Annual Poverty Report* (2024) offers an in-depth breakdown of poverty rates, demographics, and economic conditions at the county level across New York State<a href="#cite-7" title="New York State Community Action Association, 2024, https://nyscaa.memberclicks.net/assets/docs/PovReport2024/2024%20NYS%20Poverty%20Report.pdf, accessed August 2025"><sup>7</sup></a>.

*“Poverty is both a cause and consequence of poor health”*

Counties in the M-H Region continue to show wide variation in poverty rates, ranging from a low of 6.5% in Putnam County to a high of 15.6% in Rockland County. As of 2023, Rockland, Sullivan, and Ulster counties all reported poverty rates above the New York State average of 13.7%. In contrast, Putnam, Dutchess, Westchester, and Orange counties remained below the state average \[see Figure 7\].

```{python}
#| echo: false
#| warning: false
#| message: false
#| label: fig-poverty-rate
#| fig-cap: "Percentage of Population in Poverty, 2021-2023"
import sys
import os
# Add project root to path
cwd = os.getcwd()
# Try current directory first
if os.path.exists(os.path.join(cwd, 'scripts', 'cha_table_styling.py')):
    if cwd not in sys.path:
        sys.path.insert(0, cwd)
# Try parent directory (if running from chapters/)
elif os.path.exists(os.path.join(cwd, '..', 'scripts', 'cha_table_styling.py')):
    parent = os.path.abspath(os.path.join(cwd, '..'))
    if parent not in sys.path:
        sys.path.insert(0, parent)
import pandas as pd
import numpy as np
from scripts.cha_figure_builder import build_clustered_bar_figure

# Create the data
data = {
    "Year": [2021, 2022, 2023],
    "Dutchess": [8.8, 8.6, 8.3],
    "Orange": [11.7, 13.0, 13.0],
    "Putnam": [6.0, 6.3, 6.5],
    "Rockland": [14.9, 15.1, 15.6],
    "Sullivan": [14.1, 14.8, 15.2],
    "Ulster": [13.2, 14.7, 14.3],
    "Westchester": [8.2, 8.5, 8.9],
    "NYS": [13.5, 13.6, 13.7]
}

df = pd.DataFrame(data)

# Transpose the data: Counties on x-axis, Years as bars
# Set Year as index, then transpose
df_transposed = df.set_index("Year").T.reset_index()
df_transposed.rename(columns={"index": "County"}, inplace=True)

# Create clustered bar figure: x=County, bars grouped by Year
fig = build_clustered_bar_figure(
    df=df_transposed,
    x_col="County",
    y_cols=[2021, 2022, 2023],  # Years as series
    x_axis_title="County",
    y_axis_title="Poverty Rate (%)",
    start_at_zero=True,
    y_padding=0.1,
    hover_value_format=".1f",
    hover_suffix="%",
)

# Ensure categorical x-axis rendering (keeps county order)
fig.update_xaxes(type="category")

# Display the interactive figure
fig.show()
```

```{python}
#| echo: false
#| warning: false
#| message: false
#| label: tbl-poverty-rate
#| tbl-cap: "Percentage of Population in Poverty, 2021-2023"
import sys
import os
# Add project root to path
cwd = os.getcwd()
# Try current directory first
if os.path.exists(os.path.join(cwd, 'scripts', 'cha_table_styling.py')):
    if cwd not in sys.path:
        sys.path.insert(0, cwd)
# Try parent directory (if running from chapters/)
elif os.path.exists(os.path.join(cwd, '..', 'scripts', 'cha_table_styling.py')):
    parent = os.path.abspath(os.path.join(cwd, '..'))
    if parent not in sys.path:
        sys.path.insert(0, parent)
import pandas as pd
import numpy as np
from scripts.cha_table_styling import style_cha_table

# Create the data
data = {
    " ": ["2021", "2022", "2023"],
    "Dutchess": [8.8, 8.6, 8.3],
    "Orange": [11.7, 13.0, 13.0],
    "Putnam": [6.0, 6.3, 6.5],
    "Rockland": [14.9, 15.1, 15.6],
    "Sullivan": [14.1, 14.8, 15.2],
    "Ulster": [13.2, 14.7, 14.3],
    "Westchester": [8.2, 8.5, 8.9],
    "NYS": [13.5, 13.6, 13.7]
}

df = pd.DataFrame(data)

# Format the data - all are percentages
for col in df.columns:
    if col != " ":
        df[col] = df[col].apply(lambda x: f"{x:.1f}")

# Apply standard CHA styling
styled_table = style_cha_table(df)
styled_table
```

::: callout-note
## Note

The American Community Survey asks respondents their income in the past 12 months including joint income. This is for income that is received on a regular basis before payments for taxes, social security, etc. If a family's total income is less than the official poverty threshold for a family of that size and composition, they are considered to be in poverty.
:::

::: {.callout-note collapse="true"}
## Source

US Census Bureau; American Community Survey, 2023 American Community Survey 5-year estimates, [Table S1701](https://data.census.gov/table/ACSST5Y2023.S1701?q=s1701&g=050XX00US36105,36027,36071,36119,36087,36079,36111_040XX00US36), April 2025
:::

Poverty continues to vary significantly across racial and ethnic groups in the Mid-Hudson Region. Hispanic families had the highest poverty rates in Orange, Putnam, Rockland, Ulster, and Westchester counties, with Ulster County reaching 24.4%. In Sullivan County, non-Hispanic Black families experienced the highest poverty rate at 30.5%, the highest across all counties and groups. In Dutchess County, non-Hispanic Black and Hispanic families also had elevated poverty rates at 14.2% and 11.2%, respectively \[see Figure 8\].

```{python}
#| echo: false
#| warning: false
#| message: false
#| label: fig-poverty-by-race
#| fig-cap: "Percentage of Families Below Poverty Level by Race and Ethnicity, 2023" 
import sys
import os
# Add project root to path
cwd = os.getcwd()
# Try current directory first
if os.path.exists(os.path.join(cwd, 'scripts', 'cha_table_styling.py')):
    if cwd not in sys.path:
        sys.path.insert(0, cwd)
# Try parent directory (if running from chapters/)
elif os.path.exists(os.path.join(cwd, '..', 'scripts', 'cha_table_styling.py')):
    parent = os.path.abspath(os.path.join(cwd, '..'))
    if parent not in sys.path:
        sys.path.insert(0, parent)
import pandas as pd
import numpy as np
from scripts.cha_figure_builder import build_clustered_bar_figure

# Create the data
data = {
    "Race/Ethnicity": ["White (non-Hispanic)", "Black (including Hispanic)", "Asian (including Hispanic, excluding PI)", "Hispanic (any race)", "Total"],
    "Dutchess": [3.3, 14.2, 7.5, 11.2, 5.3],
    "Orange": [7.9, 12.5, 10.3, 11.3, 9.2],
    "Putnam": [2.8, 0.0, 23.4, 6.6, 3.8],
    "Rockland": [11.1, 8.6, 1.8, 12.8, 10.3],
    "Sullivan": [8.2, 30.5, 13.3, 14.3, 10.5],
    "Ulster": [4.9, 14.1, 4.8, 24.4, 7.1],
    "Westchester": [2.6, 10.8, 5.4, 10.4, 5.7],
    "NYS": [5.7, 16.5, 11.1, 17.1, 9.7]
}

df = pd.DataFrame(data)

# Transpose the data: Counties on x-axis, Race/Ethnicity as bars
# Set Race/Ethnicity as index, then transpose
df_transposed = df.set_index("Race/Ethnicity").T.reset_index()
df_transposed.rename(columns={"index": "County"}, inplace=True)

# Lock the race/ethnicity order for the bars
race_order = ["White (non-Hispanic)", "Black (including Hispanic)", "Asian (including Hispanic, excluding PI)", "Hispanic (any race)", "Total"]

# Create clustered bar figure: x=County, bars grouped by Race/Ethnicity
fig = build_clustered_bar_figure(
    df=df_transposed,
    x_col="County",
    y_cols=race_order,  # Race/Ethnicity groups as series
    x_axis_title="County",
    y_axis_title="Poverty Rate (%)",
    start_at_zero=True,
    y_padding=0.1,
    hover_value_format=".1f",
    hover_suffix="%",
)

# Ensure categorical x-axis rendering (keeps county order)
fig.update_xaxes(type="category")

# Display the interactive figure
fig.show()
```

```{python}
#| echo: false
#| warning: false
#| message: false
#| label: tbl-poverty-by-race
#| tbl-cap: "Percentage of Families Below Poverty Level by Race and Ethnicity, 2023" 
import sys
import os
# Add project root to path
cwd = os.getcwd()
# Try current directory first
if os.path.exists(os.path.join(cwd, 'scripts', 'cha_table_styling.py')):
    if cwd not in sys.path:
        sys.path.insert(0, cwd)
# Try parent directory (if running from chapters/)
elif os.path.exists(os.path.join(cwd, '..', 'scripts', 'cha_table_styling.py')):
    parent = os.path.abspath(os.path.join(cwd, '..'))
    if parent not in sys.path:
        sys.path.insert(0, parent)
import pandas as pd
import numpy as np
from scripts.cha_table_styling import style_cha_table

# Create the data
data = {
    " ": ["White (non-Hispanic)", "Black (including Hispanic)", "Asian (including Hispanic, excluding PI)", "Hispanic (any race)", "Total"],
    "Dutchess": [3.3, 14.2, 7.5, 11.2, 5.3],
    "Orange": [7.9, 12.5, 10.3, 11.3, 9.2],
    "Putnam": [2.8, 0.0, 23.4, 6.6, 3.8],
    "Rockland": [11.1, 8.6, 1.8, 12.8, 10.3],
    "Sullivan": [8.2, 30.5, 13.3, 14.3, 10.5],
    "Ulster": [4.9, 14.1, 4.8, 24.4, 7.1],
    "Westchester": [2.6, 10.8, 5.4, 10.4, 5.7],
    "NYS": [5.7, 16.5, 11.1, 17.1, 9.7]
}

df = pd.DataFrame(data)

# Format the data - all are percentages
for col in df.columns:
    if col != " ":
        df[col] = df[col].apply(lambda x: f"{x:.1f}")

# Apply standard CHA styling
styled_table = style_cha_table(df)
styled_table
```

::: callout-note
## Note

The Census Bureau collects racial and ethnic data in accordance with guidelines provided by the US Office of Management and Budget (OMB) and these data are based on self-identification. For ethnicity, the OMB standards classify individuals in one of two categories: "Hispanic or Latino" or "Not Hispanic or Latino." The Census Bureau uses the term "Hispanic or Latino" interchangeably with the term "Hispanic," and also refer to this concept as "ethnicity." People who identify with more than one race may choose to provide multiple races in response to the race question.
:::

::: {.callout-note collapse="true"}
## Source

NYS County Health Indicators by Race and Ethnicity Dashboard, June 2025 sourced from US Census Bureau, Small Area Income and Poverty Estimates

[NYS County Health Indicators](https://www.health.ny.gov/community/health_equity/reports/county/)
:::

##### Economically Disadvantaged

Children from economically disadvantaged families can face numerous challenges that influence their development, academic achievement, and overall health. Poverty can affect their cognitive development and educational attainment. The lack of access to resources and opportunities can impact their long-term social and economic mobility.

In 2023–2024, Sullivan County had the region’s highest share of economically disadvantaged students (61.4%), while Putnam had the lowest (32.5%) \[see Figure 9\]. All counties rose since 2021–2022; the largest gains occurred in Rockland (43.9% to 51.0%), Dutchess (39.2% to 43.3%), and Putnam (28.5% to 32.5%). Compared with NYS (59.2%), Sullivan is above the state average; all other Mid-Hudson counties remain below it. Ulster was higher than Orange and Rockland through 2022–2023 (52.5% vs. 49.0% and 48.1%) but dropped below both in 2023–2024 (48.8% vs. 49.5% and 51.0%).

```{python}
#| echo: false
#| warning: false
#| message: false
#| label: fig-economically-disadvantaged
#| fig-cap: "Enrollment Rate of Economically Disadvantaged Students, 2021-2024"
import sys
import os
# Add project root to path
cwd = os.getcwd()
# Try current directory first
if os.path.exists(os.path.join(cwd, 'scripts', 'cha_table_styling.py')):
    if cwd not in sys.path:
        sys.path.insert(0, cwd)
# Try parent directory (if running from chapters/)
elif os.path.exists(os.path.join(cwd, '..', 'scripts', 'cha_table_styling.py')):
    parent = os.path.abspath(os.path.join(cwd, '..'))
    if parent not in sys.path:
        sys.path.insert(0, parent)
import pandas as pd
import numpy as np
from scripts.cha_figure_builder import build_line_figure

# Create the data
data = {
    "School Year": ["2021-2022", "2022-2023", "2023-2024"],
    "Dutchess": [39.2, 43.1, 43.3],
    "Orange": [47.0, 49.0, 49.5],
    "Putnam": [28.5, 32.1, 32.5],
    "Rockland": [43.9, 48.1, 51.0],
    "Sullivan": [60.4, 62.7, 61.4],
    "Ulster": [48.6, 52.5, 48.8],
    "Westchester": [38.9, 39.6, 39.7],
    "NYS": [56.2, 59.1, 59.2]
}

df = pd.DataFrame(data)

# Create interactive Plotly figure using cha_figure_builder
fig = build_line_figure(
    df=df,
    x_col="School Year",
    x_axis_title="School Year",
    y_axis_title="Economically Disadvantaged Students (%)",
    start_at_zero=False,
    y_padding=0.1,
    hover_value_format=".1f",
    hover_suffix="%",
)

# Display the interactive figure
fig.show()
```

```{python}
#| echo: false
#| warning: false
#| message: false
#| label: tbl-economically-disadvantaged
#| tbl-cap: "Enrollment Rate of Economically Disadvantaged Students, 2021-2024"
import sys
import os
# Add project root to path
cwd = os.getcwd()
# Try current directory first
if os.path.exists(os.path.join(cwd, 'scripts', 'cha_table_styling.py')):
    if cwd not in sys.path:
        sys.path.insert(0, cwd)
# Try parent directory (if running from chapters/)
elif os.path.exists(os.path.join(cwd, '..', 'scripts', 'cha_table_styling.py')):
    parent = os.path.abspath(os.path.join(cwd, '..'))
    if parent not in sys.path:
        sys.path.insert(0, parent)
import pandas as pd
import numpy as np
from scripts.cha_table_styling import style_cha_table

# Create the data
data = {
    " ": ["2021-2022", "2022-2023", "2023-2024"],
    "Dutchess": [39.2, 43.1, 43.3],
    "Orange": [47.0, 49.0, 49.5],
    "Putnam": [28.5, 32.1, 32.5],
    "Rockland": [43.9, 48.1, 51.0],
    "Sullivan": [60.4, 62.7, 61.4],
    "Ulster": [48.6, 52.5, 48.8],
    "Westchester": [38.9, 39.6, 39.7],
    "NYS": [56.2, 59.1, 59.2]
}

df = pd.DataFrame(data)

# Format the data - all are percentages
for col in df.columns:
    if col != " ":
        df[col] = df[col].apply(lambda x: f"{x:.1f}")

# Apply standard CHA styling
styled_table = style_cha_table(df)
styled_table
```

::: callout-note
## Note

Students who participate in, or whose family participates in, economic assistance programs, such as the Free or Reduced-Price Lunch Programs; Social Security Insurance; Supplemental Nutrition Assistance Program; Foster Care; Refugee Assistance (cash or medical assistance); Earned Income Tax Credit; Home Energy Assistance Program; Safety Net Assistance; Bureau of Indian Affairs; or Family Assistance: Temporary Assistance for Needy Families. If one student in a family is identified as low income, all students from that household (economic unit) may be identified as low income.
:::

::: {.callout-note collapse="true"}
## Source

NYS Education Department, June 2025

[NYS Education Data](https://data.nysed.gov/enrollment.php?year=2024&county=13)
:::

#### Asset Limited, Income Constrained, Employed (ALICE)

Asset Limited, Income Constrained, Employed (ALICE) households are those earning above the Federal Poverty Level but below the ALICE Threshold, the income needed to afford the basics where they live, as calculated in the Household Survival Budget. The ALICE measure is location and household-specific and covers six essentials: housing, childcare, food, transportation, health care, and technology. These constraints force households to make difficult trade-offs across these necessities, see Table 15 for a sample budget.

The 2023 ALICE Household Survival Budget for New York State shows that the income needed to cover basic necessities ranges from \$35,652 per year for a single adult (\$17.80/hour) to \$104,472 for two adults with two children in childcare (\$52.20/hour). A two-adult, two-child household without paid childcare requires \$83,664 annually. Housing and childcare are the largest drivers for families with two children in care, those two items total \$3,782 per month. These amounts define the ALICE Threshold; county-level percentages below this threshold. \[see Table 15\]



```{python}
#| echo: false
#| warning: false
#| message: false
#| label: tbl-alice-budget
#| tbl-cap: "ALICE Household Survival Budget, New York State, 2023"
import sys
import os
# Add project root to path
cwd = os.getcwd()
# Try current directory first
if os.path.exists(os.path.join(cwd, 'scripts', 'cha_table_styling.py')):
    if cwd not in sys.path:
        sys.path.insert(0, cwd)
# Try parent directory (if running from chapters/)
elif os.path.exists(os.path.join(cwd, '..', 'scripts', 'cha_table_styling.py')):
    parent = os.path.abspath(os.path.join(cwd, '..'))
    if parent not in sys.path:
        sys.path.insert(0, parent)
import pandas as pd
import numpy as np
from scripts.cha_table_styling import style_cha_table

# Create the data
data = {
    " ": ["Housing", "Child Care", "Food", "Transportation", "Health Care", "Technology", "Miscellaneous", "Taxes", "Monthly Total", "ANNUAL TOTAL", "Hourly Wage"],
    "Single Adult": [1103, 0, 516, 401, 196, 86, 230, 439, 2971, 35652, 17.83],
    "One Adult, One Child": [1189, 423, 873, 535, 452, 86, 356, 461, 4375, 52500, 26.25],
    "One Adult, One in Child Care": [1189, 1126, 783, 506, 452, 86, 414, 625, 5181, 62172, 31.09],
    "Two Adults": [1189, 0, 946, 617, 452, 116, 332, 529, 4181, 50172, 25.09],
    "Two Adults, Two Children": [1437, 844, 1587, 957, 775, 116, 572, 684, 6972, 83664, 41.83],
    "Two Adults, Two in Child Care": [1437, 2345, 1400, 899, 775, 116, 697, 1037, 8706, 104472, 52.24],
    "Single Adult 65+": [1103, 0, 475, 346, 543, 86, 255, 510, 3318, 39816, 19.91],
    "Two Adults 65+": [1189, 0, 870, 508, 1086, 116, 377, 861, 5007, 60084, 30.04]
}

df = pd.DataFrame(data)

# Format the data - currency for most columns, percentages for hourly wage
for col in df.columns:
    if col != " ":
        if col == "Hourly Wage":
            df[col] = df[col].apply(lambda x: f"${x:.2f}")
        else:
            # Check if it's a dollar amount (all rows except Hourly Wage)
            df[col] = df[col].apply(lambda x: f"${x:,.0f}" if isinstance(x, (int, float)) and x >= 100 else f"${x:.0f}" if isinstance(x, (int, float)) else x)

# Apply standard CHA styling
styled_table = style_cha_table(df)
styled_table
```

::: callout-note
## Note

The ALICE (Asset Limited, Income Constrained, Employed) household survival budget estimates the minimum cost of household necessities (housing, childcare, food, transportation, health care, and technology) plus taxes, and a contingency fund (miscellaneous) equal to 10% of the budget.
:::

::: {.callout-note collapse="true"}
## Source

United for ALICE, June 2025

[ALICE Cost of Basics](https://www.unitedforalice.org/the-cost-of-basics/new-york)
:::

Across the M-H Region, the share of households below the ALICE Threshold ranges from 37% in Dutchess (lowest) to 50% in Rockland (highest). Rockland is the only county above the New York State average of 47%; Orange (43%), Sullivan (45%), Ulster (42%), Putnam (38%), Westchester (38%), and Dutchess (37%) all fall below the state average. Sullivan has the region’s highest poverty rate (15%), while Rockland has the highest ALICE share (38%), which drives its overall total. In every county, ALICE households outnumber those in poverty, often by two to five times, showing that many financially constrained households earn above the Federal Poverty Level but still cannot afford basic needs \[see Figure 10\].

```{python}
#| echo: false
#| warning: false
#| message: false
#| label: fig-alice-threshold
#| fig-cap: "ALICE Threshold Percentage, 2023"
import sys
import os
# Add project root to path
cwd = os.getcwd()
# Try current directory first
if os.path.exists(os.path.join(cwd, 'scripts', 'cha_table_styling.py')):
    if cwd not in sys.path:
        sys.path.insert(0, cwd)
# Try parent directory (if running from chapters/)
elif os.path.exists(os.path.join(cwd, '..', 'scripts', 'cha_table_styling.py')):
    parent = os.path.abspath(os.path.join(cwd, '..'))
    if parent not in sys.path:
        sys.path.insert(0, parent)
import pandas as pd
import numpy as np
from scripts.cha_figure_builder import build_stacked_bar_figure

# Create the data
data = {
    "Category": ["ALICE", "Poverty", "Above ALICE Threshold"],
    "Dutchess": [31.0, 6.0, 62.0],
    "Orange": [33.0, 10.0, 57.0],
    "Putnam": [32.0, 6.0, 63.0],
    "Rockland": [38.0, 12.0, 50.0],
    "Sullivan": [30.0, 15.0, 54.0],
    "Ulster": [32.0, 10.0, 58.0],
    "Westchester": [28.0, 10.0, 61.0],
    "NYS": [33.0, 14.0, 52.0]
}

df = pd.DataFrame(data)

# Transpose: County on x-axis, Categories as stacked series
df_transposed = df.set_index("Category").T.reset_index()
df_transposed.columns.name = None
df_transposed = df_transposed.rename(columns={"index": "County"})

# Lock the category order for stacking
category_order = ["ALICE", "Poverty", "Above ALICE Threshold"]
category_cols = [col for col in df_transposed.columns if col != "County"]

# Lock the county order
county_order = [c for c in df.columns if c != "Category"]
df_transposed["County"] = pd.Categorical(df_transposed["County"], categories=county_order, ordered=True)
df_transposed = df_transposed.sort_values("County")

# Create stacked bar figure: x=County, bars stacked by Category
fig = build_stacked_bar_figure(
    df=df_transposed,
    x_col="County",
    y_cols=category_cols,  # Categories as stacked series
    x_axis_title="County",
    y_axis_title="Percentage of Households (%)",
    start_at_zero=True,
    y_padding=0.1,
    hover_value_format=".1f",
    hover_suffix="%",
)

# Ensure categorical x-axis rendering (keeps county order)
fig.update_xaxes(type="category")

# Display the interactive figure
fig.show()
```

```{python}
#| echo: false
#| warning: false
#| message: false
#| label: tbl-alice-threshold
#| tbl-cap: "ALICE Threshold Percentage, 2023"
import sys
import os
# Add project root to path
cwd = os.getcwd()
# Try current directory first
if os.path.exists(os.path.join(cwd, 'scripts', 'cha_table_styling.py')):
    if cwd not in sys.path:
        sys.path.insert(0, cwd)
# Try parent directory (if running from chapters/)
elif os.path.exists(os.path.join(cwd, '..', 'scripts', 'cha_table_styling.py')):
    parent = os.path.abspath(os.path.join(cwd, '..'))
    if parent not in sys.path:
        sys.path.insert(0, parent)
import pandas as pd
import numpy as np
from scripts.cha_table_styling import style_cha_table

# Create the data
data = {
    " ": ["ALICE", "Poverty", "Above ALICE Threshold"],
    "Dutchess": [31.0, 6.0, 62.0],
    "Orange": [33.0, 10.0, 57.0],
    "Putnam": [32.0, 6.0, 63.0],
    "Rockland": [38.0, 12.0, 50.0],
    "Sullivan": [30.0, 15.0, 54.0],
    "Ulster": [32.0, 10.0, 58.0],
    "Westchester": [28.0, 10.0, 61.0],
    "NYS": [33.0, 14.0, 52.0]
}

df = pd.DataFrame(data)

# Format the data - all are percentages
for col in df.columns:
    if col != " ":
        df[col] = df[col].apply(lambda x: f"{x:.1f}")

# Apply standard CHA styling
styled_table = style_cha_table(df)
styled_table
```

::: callout-note
## Note

The ALICE (Asset Limited, Income Constrained, Employed) threshold represents the percentage of households in the United States that are struggling financially, falling below a threshold that includes those in poverty and those who are asset limited, income constrained, and employed. ALICE households earn above the Federal Poverty Level, but not enough to afford the basic necessities in their communities. Nationally, over 40% of households fall below the ALICE threshold.
:::

::: {.callout-note collapse="true"}
## Source

United for ALICE, June 2025

[ALICE County Reports](https://www.unitedforalice.org/county-reports/new-york#10/41.1237/-73.7330)
:::

## Education

### High School Graduation

High school completion is strongly linked to better health and longevity. People who don’t complete high school face limited employment prospects, lower wages, and a higher risk of poverty factors that contribute to worse health outcomes and a higher risk of death later in life. Graduation likelihood is shaped by individual, family, school, and community conditions; school climate and safety, access to supportive adults, and disciplinary practices are all associated with on-time graduation.

In 2023, graduation rates in the M-H Region ranged from 76% in Sullivan (lowest) to 91% in Putnam and Westchester (highest). Most counties exceeded the NYS average of 86%, Dutchess (87%), Orange (89%), Putnam (91%), Ulster (87%), and Westchester (91%), while Rockland matched the state rate (86%) and Sullivan fell below. From 2021–2023, rates were generally stable or edged down slightly, with the largest declines in Sullivan (82% to 76%), Rockland (90% to 86%), and Putnam (94% to 91%). \[see Figure 11\]

```{python}
#| echo: false
#| warning: false
#| message: false
#| label: fig-graduation-rate
#| fig-cap: "High School Graduation Rate, 2021-2023"
import sys
import os
# Add project root to path
cwd = os.getcwd()
# Try current directory first
if os.path.exists(os.path.join(cwd, 'scripts', 'cha_table_styling.py')):
    if cwd not in sys.path:
        sys.path.insert(0, cwd)
# Try parent directory (if running from chapters/)
elif os.path.exists(os.path.join(cwd, '..', 'scripts', 'cha_table_styling.py')):
    parent = os.path.abspath(os.path.join(cwd, '..'))
    if parent not in sys.path:
        sys.path.insert(0, parent)
import pandas as pd
import numpy as np
from scripts.cha_figure_builder import build_clustered_bar_figure

# Create the data
data = {
    "Year": [2021, 2022, 2023],
    "Dutchess": [87.0, 86.0, 87.0],
    "Orange": [89.0, 89.0, 89.0],
    "Putnam": [94.0, 94.0, 91.0],
    "Rockland": [90.0, 88.0, 86.0],
    "Sullivan": [82.0, 78.0, 76.0],
    "Ulster": [87.0, 87.0, 87.0],
    "Westchester": [91.0, 92.0, 91.0],
    "NYS": [86.0, 87.0, 86.0]
}

df = pd.DataFrame(data)

# Pivot data: County on x-axis, Year as separate series (columns)
df_long = df.melt(id_vars="Year", var_name="County", value_name="Graduation Rate")
df_pivoted = df_long.pivot(index="County", columns="Year", values="Graduation Rate").reset_index()

# Lock the county order
county_order = [c for c in df.columns if c != "Year"]
df_pivoted["County"] = pd.Categorical(df_pivoted["County"], categories=county_order, ordered=True)
df_pivoted = df_pivoted.sort_values("County")

# Create clustered bar figure: x=County, bars grouped by Year
year_cols = sorted([col for col in df_pivoted.columns if col != "County"])
fig = build_clustered_bar_figure(
    df=df_pivoted,
    x_col="County",
    y_cols=year_cols,  # Years as separate series (bars grouped by year)
    x_axis_title="County",
    y_axis_title="High School Graduation Rate (%)",
    start_at_zero=False,
    y_padding=0.1,
    hover_value_format=".1f",
    hover_suffix="%",
)

# Ensure categorical x-axis rendering (keeps county order)
fig.update_xaxes(type="category")

# Display the interactive figure
fig.show()
```

```{python}
#| echo: false
#| warning: false
#| message: false
#| label: tbl-graduation-rate
#| tbl-cap: "High School Graduation Rate, 2021-2023"
import sys
import os
# Add project root to path
cwd = os.getcwd()
# Try current directory first
if os.path.exists(os.path.join(cwd, 'scripts', 'cha_table_styling.py')):
    if cwd not in sys.path:
        sys.path.insert(0, cwd)
# Try parent directory (if running from chapters/)
elif os.path.exists(os.path.join(cwd, '..', 'scripts', 'cha_table_styling.py')):
    parent = os.path.abspath(os.path.join(cwd, '..'))
    if parent not in sys.path:
        sys.path.insert(0, parent)
import pandas as pd
import numpy as np
from scripts.cha_table_styling import style_cha_table

# Create the data
data = {
    " ": ["2021", "2022", "2023"],
    "Dutchess": [87.0, 86.0, 87.0],
    "Orange": [89.0, 89.0, 89.0],
    "Putnam": [94.0, 94.0, 91.0],
    "Rockland": [90.0, 88.0, 86.0],
    "Sullivan": [82.0, 78.0, 76.0],
    "Ulster": [87.0, 87.0, 87.0],
    "Westchester": [91.0, 92.0, 91.0],
    "NYS": [86.0, 87.0, 86.0]
}

df = pd.DataFrame(data)

# Format the data - all are percentages
for col in df.columns:
    if col != " ":
        df[col] = df[col].apply(lambda x: f"{x:.1f}")

# Apply standard CHA styling
styled_table = style_cha_table(df)
styled_table
```

::: callout-note
## Note

Graduation rate data are reported for a 9th grade cohort, as of the 4th year of high school - August. Graduates include students who received a local diploma or a local diploma with Regents endorsement (Regents diploma).
:::

::: {.callout-note collapse="true"}
## Source

NYS Education Department, June 2025

[NYS Education Data](https://data.nysed.gov/lists.php?type=county)
:::

In accordance with federal regulation, there is a two-part requirement regarding racial and ethnic designation. First, all students must be reported as Hispanic/Latino or not Hispanic/Latino. Second, all students must be reported with at least one race. Students who are reported as Hispanic/Latino, regardless of their race, will be counted as Hispanic/Latino for reporting purposes. Students who are reported as not Hispanic/Latino will be counted in the race category in which they are reported. Non-Hispanic students who are reported with more than one race category will be reported as Multiracial. 

Racial and ethnic disparities in graduation rates persist across the M-H Region. In six of seven counties, White students graduate at higher rates than both Black and Hispanic students; the exception is Putnam, where Black students post the region’s highest rate (97%), exceeding White (95%) and Hispanic (80%) peers. The largest gaps occur between White and Hispanic students, 21 points in Sullivan (84% vs. 63%) and then 15 points in Putnam (95% vs 80%) and Rockland (96% vs. 81%). Disparities between White and Black students are greatest in Sullivan (18 points; 84% vs. 66%) and Dutchess (14 points; 92% vs. 78%), while Rockland (96% vs 90%) and Ulster (90% vs 84%) show one of the smallest White and Black gaps at 6 points. \[see Figure 12\]

```{python}
#| echo: false
#| warning: false
#| message: false
#| label: fig-graduation-by-race
#| fig-cap: "High School Graduation Rate, by Race and Ethnicity, 2023"
import sys
import os
# Add project root to path
cwd = os.getcwd()
# Try current directory first
if os.path.exists(os.path.join(cwd, 'scripts', 'cha_table_styling.py')):
    if cwd not in sys.path:
        sys.path.insert(0, cwd)
# Try parent directory (if running from chapters/)
elif os.path.exists(os.path.join(cwd, '..', 'scripts', 'cha_table_styling.py')):
    parent = os.path.abspath(os.path.join(cwd, '..'))
    if parent not in sys.path:
        sys.path.insert(0, parent)
import pandas as pd
import numpy as np
from scripts.cha_figure_builder import build_clustered_bar_figure

# Create the data - use np.nan for suppressed data
data = {
    "Race/Ethnicity": ["Asian or Native Hawaiian/Other PI", "Black or African American", "White", "Multiracial", "Hispanic"],
    "Dutchess": [91.0, 78.0, 92.0, 80.0, 79.0],
    "Orange": [96.0, 85.0, 93.0, 88.0, 83.0],
    "Putnam": [100.0, 97.0, 95.0, np.nan, 80.0],
    "Rockland": [97.0, 86.0, 94.0, np.nan, 74.0],
    "Sullivan": [np.nan, 66.0, 84.0, 68.0, 63.0],
    "Ulster": [np.nan, 84.0, 90.0, 74.0, 80.0],
    "Westchester": [97.0, 83.0, 96.0, 94.0, 85.0],
    "NYS": [93.0, 81.0, 91.0, 84.0, 81.0]
}

df = pd.DataFrame(data)

# Lock the race/ethnicity order
race_order = ["Asian or Native Hawaiian/Other PI", "Black or African American", "White", "Multiracial", "Hispanic"]
df["Race/Ethnicity"] = pd.Categorical(df["Race/Ethnicity"], categories=race_order, ordered=True)
df = df.sort_values("Race/Ethnicity")

# Create clustered bar figure: x=Race/Ethnicity, bars grouped by County
fig = build_clustered_bar_figure(
    df=df,
    x_col="Race/Ethnicity",
    y_cols=[col for col in df.columns if col != "Race/Ethnicity"],  # All counties as series
    x_axis_title="Race/Ethnicity",
    y_axis_title="High School Graduation Rate (%)",
    start_at_zero=True,
    y_padding=0.1,
    hover_value_format=".1f",
    hover_suffix="%",
)

# Ensure categorical x-axis rendering (keeps race/ethnicity order)
fig.update_xaxes(type="category")

# Display the interactive figure
fig.show()
```

```{python}
#| echo: false
#| warning: false
#| message: false
#| label: tbl-graduation-by-race
#| tbl-cap: "High School Graduation Rate, by Race and Ethnicity, 2023"
import sys
import os
# Add project root to path
cwd = os.getcwd()
# Try current directory first
if os.path.exists(os.path.join(cwd, 'scripts', 'cha_table_styling.py')):
    if cwd not in sys.path:
        sys.path.insert(0, cwd)
# Try parent directory (if running from chapters/)
elif os.path.exists(os.path.join(cwd, '..', 'scripts', 'cha_table_styling.py')):
    parent = os.path.abspath(os.path.join(cwd, '..'))
    if parent not in sys.path:
        sys.path.insert(0, parent)
import pandas as pd
import numpy as np
from scripts.cha_table_styling import style_cha_table

# Create the data - use np.nan for suppressed data
data = {
    " ": ["Asian or Native Hawaiian/Other PI", "Black or African American", "White", "Multiracial", "Hispanic"],
    "Dutchess": [91.0, 78.0, 92.0, 80.0, 79.0],
    "Orange": [96.0, 85.0, 93.0, 88.0, 83.0],
    "Putnam": [100.0, 97.0, 95.0, np.nan, 80.0],
    "Rockland": [97.0, 86.0, 94.0, np.nan, 74.0],
    "Sullivan": [np.nan, 66.0, 84.0, 68.0, 63.0],
    "Ulster": [np.nan, 84.0, 90.0, 74.0, 80.0],
    "Westchester": [97.0, 83.0, 96.0, 94.0, 85.0],
    "NYS": [93.0, 81.0, 91.0, 84.0, 81.0]
}

df = pd.DataFrame(data)

# Format the data - all are percentages, handle suppressed data
for col in df.columns:
    if col != " ":
        df[col] = df[col].apply(lambda x: "s" if pd.isna(x) else f"{x:.1f}")

# Apply standard CHA styling
styled_table = style_cha_table(df)
styled_table
```

::: callout-note
## Note

s: Data are suppressed due to not meeting reporting criteria. Race or races with which the student primarily identifies are indicated by the student or the parent/guardian.
:::

::: {.callout-note collapse="true"}
## Source

NYS Education Department, June 2025

[NYS Graduation Rate Data](https://data.nysed.gov/gradrate.php?year=2023&state=yes)
:::

### Early Childhood Education and Development

The early years of a child's life are very important for their health and development. WHO's 2024 handbook operationalizes Nurturing Care what to deliver (parenting content), where to deliver it (health/social/education platforms), and how to deliver it well (training, quality assurance, M&E, and scaling)<a href="#cite-8" title="World Health Organization. Designing, Implementing, Evaluating, and Scaling Up Parenting Interventions: A Handbook for Decision-Makers and Implementers. Geneva: WHO, 2024, https://iris.who.int/bitstream/handle/10665/378237/9789240095595-eng.pdf, accessed August 2025"><sup>8</sup></a>. *WHO* characterizes nurturing care as a stable environment that promotes health and optimal nutrition, protects children from threats, and gives them opportunities for early learning, through affectionate interactions and relationships. Components of nurturing care include adequate nutrition, responsive caregiving, security and safety, opportunities for early learning, and good health.

Early-life stress and adverse events are linked to lasting mental and physical health impacts. Stressors, including poverty, physical abuse, family instability, and unsafe neighborhoods are associated with inadequate coping skills, difficulty regulating emotions, and reduced social functioning.

### Adverse Childhood Experiences

Adverse Childhood Experiences (ACEs) are potentially traumatic events that occur during childhood such as “experiencing violence, abuse, or neglect; having a family member attempt or die by suicide; and witnessing violence in the home.” Elements of a child’s environment that weaken their sense of safety, stability, and bonding such as substance misuse, mental health complications, or family instability (including divorce or incarceration of parents and relatives) contribute to ACEs. ACEs can have lasting effects on health, behavior, and life potential, including obesity, diabetes, depression, suicide attempts, sexually transmitted infections (STIs), heart disease, cancer, stroke, Chronic Obstructive Pulmonary Disease (COPD), broken bones, smoking, alcoholism, drug use, graduation rates, academic achievement, lost time from work, etc. Growing research shows that toxic stress as a result of ACEs can damage “the most basic levels of the nervous, endocrine, and immune system,” and can modify the physical structure of DNA.

The ACE items are a surveillance tool, not a diagnosis. They estimate how common early-life adversities are among adults. The questionnaire includes questions about: physical, emotional and sexual abuse; physical and emotional neglect; and household dysfunction (substance use, mental illness, mother treated violence, parental separation or divorce, and incarcerated household member). Each item screens for a *type* of adversity before age 18. When combined, they produce an ACE count (how many types a person reports), which is summarized as~~,~~ a threshold associated with measurably higher risk for poor health, mental health, and social outcomes across the life span.

### Attainment of Higher Education

Continuing education after high school, especially enrolling in and completing college, improves employment prospects and job quality, reduces the risk of unemployment or underemployment, and raises lifetime earnings<a href="#cite-9" title="Social Security Administration. Research Summary: Education and Lifetime Earnings, 2015, https://www.ssa.gov/policy/docs/research-summaries/education-earnings.html, accessed August 2025"><sup>9</sup></a>. A Social Security Administration analysis linking survey and administrative earnings records estimates that men with a bachelor's degree earn about \$900,000 more in median lifetime earnings than high school graduates and women about \$630,000 more; for graduate degrees, the gaps rise to \$1.5 million (men) and \$1.1 million (women). Even after adjusting for key socio-demographic factors, differences remain substantial (about \$655,000 for men and \$450,000 for women with a bachelor’s degree). These gains translate into better-paying jobs with fewer safety hazards and greater access to material and psychosocial resources (e.g., higher-quality housing, higher social status) and are associated with improved health and a lower risk of premature death.

Westchester leads the region in degree completion, with the highest shares of residents holding a bachelor’s (26.0%) and a graduate/professional degree (26.5%), both above NYS (22.0% and 17.5%). Putnam (24.2%) and Rockland (23.2%) also exceed the state for bachelor’s attainment, while Dutchess (21.0%), Ulster (20.2%), Orange (18.2%), and Sullivan (17.0%) fall below. \[see Figure 13\]

Figure 13

```{python}
#| echo: false
#| warning: false
#| message: false
#| label: fig-educational-attainment
#| fig-cap: "Rate of Education Attainment, 2023"
import sys
import os
# Add project root to path
cwd = os.getcwd()
# Try current directory first
if os.path.exists(os.path.join(cwd, 'scripts', 'cha_table_styling.py')):
    if cwd not in sys.path:
        sys.path.insert(0, cwd)
# Try parent directory (if running from chapters/)
elif os.path.exists(os.path.join(cwd, '..', 'scripts', 'cha_table_styling.py')):
    parent = os.path.abspath(os.path.join(cwd, '..'))
    if parent not in sys.path:
        sys.path.insert(0, parent)
import pandas as pd
import numpy as np
from scripts.cha_figure_builder import build_stacked_bar_figure

# Create the data
data = {
    "Education Level": ["Some college, no degree", "Associate's degree", "Bachelor's degree", "Graduate or professional degree"],
    "Dutchess": [16.9, 10.1, 21.0, 18.7],
    "Orange": [19.1, 10.3, 18.2, 14.1],
    "Putnam": [16.5, 8.4, 24.2, 19.3],
    "Rockland": [16.2, 7.8, 23.2, 18.9],
    "Sullivan": [17.9, 10.3, 17.0, 12.7],
    "Ulster": [17.1, 9.6, 20.2, 16.9],
    "Westchester": [12.6, 6.4, 26.0, 26.5],
    "NYS": [14.9, 8.9, 22.0, 17.5]
}

df = pd.DataFrame(data)

# Lock the education level order
edu_order = ["Some college, no degree", "Associate's degree", "Bachelor's degree", "Graduate or professional degree"]
df["Education Level"] = pd.Categorical(df["Education Level"], categories=edu_order, ordered=True)
df = df.sort_values("Education Level")

# Create stacked bar figure: x=Education Level, bars stacked by County
fig = build_stacked_bar_figure(
    df=df,
    x_col="Education Level",
    y_cols=[col for col in df.columns if col != "Education Level"],  # All counties as series
    x_axis_title="Education Level",
    y_axis_title="Percentage (%)",
    start_at_zero=True,
    y_padding=0.1,
    hover_value_format=".1f",
    hover_suffix="%",
)

# Ensure categorical x-axis rendering (keeps education level order)
fig.update_xaxes(type="category")

# Display the interactive figure
fig.show()
```

```{python}
#| echo: false
#| warning: false
#| message: false
#| label: tbl-educational-attainment
#| tbl-cap: "Rate of Education Attainment, 2023"
import sys
import os
# Add project root to path
cwd = os.getcwd()
# Try current directory first
if os.path.exists(os.path.join(cwd, 'scripts', 'cha_table_styling.py')):
    if cwd not in sys.path:
        sys.path.insert(0, cwd)
# Try parent directory (if running from chapters/)
elif os.path.exists(os.path.join(cwd, '..', 'scripts', 'cha_table_styling.py')):
    parent = os.path.abspath(os.path.join(cwd, '..'))
    if parent not in sys.path:
        sys.path.insert(0, parent)
import pandas as pd
import numpy as np
from scripts.cha_table_styling import style_cha_table

# Create the data
data = {
    " ": ["Some college, no degree", "Associate's degree", "Bachelor's degree", "Graduate or professional degree"],
    "Dutchess": [16.9, 10.1, 21.0, 18.7],
```
